title: NIS2 Effectiveness Assessment of Cybersecurity Measures
exclusions: Does not define which metrics constitute adequate effectiveness under Art.21(2)(f), set MTTR/MTTF targets, or replace management judgement on whether improvement trends are sufficient. Only trends response-time data from compliance-tagged notables.
evidence: Saved search `UC-22.2.9` extracts, dashboard PNG/PDF exports, and `index=audit_evidence` payloads referencing `measure`, `pct`, and `status` for NIS2 effectiveness monitoring.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.21(2)(f) requires policies to assess the effectiveness of cybersecurity risk-management measures. The UC trends MTTR and MTTF for compliance-tagged ES notables over quarterly windows, proving the entity measures effectiveness. Whether the metrics drive actual improvement remains a governance decision outside Splunk, hence partial.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(f) (Policies and procedures effectiveness) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.9: NIS2 Effectiveness Assessment of Cybersecurity Measures.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.2.9' running on index=risk, index=vulnerability, index=itsi_summary, _audit, _internal, CIM data models, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: For a chosen week, lower the Tenable SLA branch by ingesting a test host with `severity=High`, `first_found` older than 45 days, and `state=Active`, then run the search: `Patch_SLA_Compliance` must show `status=FAIL` while other measures still calculate.
controlTest.negativeScenario: After remediating that fixture host and rerunning Tenable so `state` is Resolved, or adjusting `first_found` inside policy SLAs, rerun over `-30d`; expect zero FAIL rows when all branches meet targets.

dataSources: `index=grc` `sourcetype=grc:risk` carries `risk_id`, `treatment_status`, `review_due`, `risk_owner`; `index=itsm` `sourcetype=snow:sn_grc_policy` carries `policy_number`, `approval_status`, `next_review_date` (Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow, Splunkbase 1928). `index=vuln` `sourcetype=tenable:vuln` includes `severity`, `first_found`, `state`; `index=pentest` `sourcetype=pentest:finding` includes `finding_id`, `remediation_due`. MFA coverage proxy uses `index=_internal` `sourcetype=splunk_web_access` with `user`. Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263) and Splunk ITSI (Splunkbase 1841) contextualise service outcomes via `index=itsi_summary` fields `health_score`, `service_name`.
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk ITSI (Splunkbase 1841), Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928), Tenable Add-On for Splunk (Splunkbase 4060)
spl:
| makeresults
| eval measure="MFA_Coverage"
| append [
    search index=_internal sourcetype=splunk_web_access earliest=-24h
    | stats dc(user) as total_users dc(eval(if(match(_raw,"(?i)mfa|2fa|totp"),user,null()))) as mfa_users
    | eval measure="MFA_Coverage", pct=round(100*mfa_users/total_users,1), target=95
]
| append [
    search index=vuln sourcetype="tenable:vuln" state="Active" earliest=-30d
    | eval sla_days=case(severity="Critical",7, severity="High",30, 1=1,90)
    | eval age_days=round((now()-first_found)/86400,1)
    | eval in_sla=if(age_days<=sla_days,1,0)
    | stats avg(in_sla) as pct_raw
    | eval measure="Patch_SLA_Compliance", pct=round(pct_raw*100,1), target=90
]
| append [
    search index=itsi_summary is_service_in_maintenance=0 earliest=-7d
    | stats avg(eval(if(health_score>=70,1,0))) as pct_raw by service_name
    | stats avg(pct_raw) as pct_raw
    | eval measure="Service_Availability", pct=round(pct_raw*100,1), target=99
]
| where isnotnull(pct)
| eval status=if(pct>=target,"PASS","FAIL")
| table measure, pct, target, status
description: Rolls up measurable signals—login hardening proxy from web UI patterns, open vulnerability remediation pace against tiered SLAs, and IT service health scores—into pass/fail KPI tiles so teams can show that security controls are working in practice, not only documented.
value: Cuts audit prep time by giving risk and compliance leaders one scheduled export that ties everyday operations (patch queues, service health, workforce sign-in posture) to the effectiveness story regulators expect, with timestamps suitable for long-term evidence stores.
implementation: (1) Confirm `index=vuln` and `index=itsi_summary` are populated; map Tenable fields per TA (Splunkbase 4060). (2) If grc:risk or snow:sn_grc_policy feeds exist (Splunkbase 1928), add a second dashboard panel for treatment-age—not this base SPL. (3) Save as `UC-22.2.9` with cron monthly plus on-demand for board packs. (4) Route FAIL rows to the CISO office channel; throttle per `measure`. (5) Archive CSV/PDF outputs to `index=audit_evidence` with signing per `evidenceSigning`.
visualization: KPI tiles (pass/fail per measure), gauges (% vs target), sortable table of failing measures, line chart of rolling quarter trends.
knownFalsePositives:
• **Splunk Web MFA keyword noise** — Distinguish: help-desk articles or marketing pages in `_raw` mention “MFA” while `user` is anonymous. Suppress: filter `sourcetype=splunk_web_access` with `uri_path` allow-list via lookup `nis2_splunk_ui_allowlist.csv`.
• **Tenable scan gaps after network freeze** — Distinguish: `first_found` stops advancing because scans paused, not because patches failed. Suppress: join `index=maintenance` or time-bound exception rows in `nis2_exception_register.csv` keyed on `maintenance_window_id`.
• **ITSI maintenance flag forgotten** — Distinguish: `is_service_in_maintenance=0` but operations declared outage in ticketing. Suppress: enrich with `lookup snow:task` for `u_outage_active` and `where u_outage_active!="true"`.
• **Small-office Splunk user bases** — Distinguish: `total_users` under ~20 makes MFA percentages jumpy. Suppress: add `where total_users>=50` except for hosts listed in lookup `nis2_micro_office_hosts.csv` with `suppress_mfa_kpi=true`.
• **Duplicate vulnerability records** — Distinguish: multiple `plugin_id` rows per asset from duplicate agents. Suppress: pre-stats `dedup host plugin_id` in a macro before SLA math.
equipmentModels: Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services (Splunkbase 3110, Entra ID MFA usage telemetry)
Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928, GRC policy and risk assessment data)
Splunk Enterprise / Splunk Cloud (REST API for saved search audit, _internal for scheduler health)
Splunk Enterprise Security 7.x (Splunkbase 263, correlation search performance and notable event data)
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Technology Add-on**: Tenable Add-on for Splunk (Splunkbase app ID 4060) must be installed on search heads and indexers. Verify with `| rest /services/apps/local | search label="Tenable Add-on for Splunk" | table label version`.
• **Indexes**: `_internal`, `vuln`, `itsi_summary` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.21(2)(f)`. The control objective is: "Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(f) (Policies and procedures effectiveness) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.9: NIS2 Effectiveness Assessment of Cybersecurity Measures." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is performed by the Tenable Add-on for Splunk (Splunkbase app ID 4060), landing events in `_internal`, `vuln`, `itsi_summary` under `splunk_web_access`, `tenable:vuln`. Daily vulnerability deltas for a 10000-asset estate produce 50000-500000 findings/day (~5-50 GB/month). Full snapshots weekly add more.

Configure the input through Settings > Data Inputs > Tenable.sc Vulnerability. A reference `inputs.conf` stanza looks like:

```ini
[tenable_sc_vuln://prod]
endpoint = https://tenable.example.com
interval = 86400
index = vulnerability
sourcetype = tenable:sc:vuln
```

The following fields are authoritative for this detection once the TA parses them: `pluginID, severity, state, first_seen, last_seen, host, dns, ip, plugin_name, cve`. Verify they are extracted at search time with `index=_internal earliest=-15m | fieldsummary` before scheduling the detection.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Confirm `index=vuln` and `index=itsi_summary` are populated; map Tenable fields per TA (Splunkbase 4060). This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** If grc:risk or snow:sn_grc_policy feeds exist (Splunkbase 1928), add a second dashboard panel for treatment-age—not this base SPL. Ingestion SLA drift is usually invisible until it breaks something — monitor the feed's freshness with a companion search that alerts when the newest event is older than the expected cadence.

• **Hint 3:** Save as `UC-22.2.9` with cron monthly plus on-demand for board packs. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** Route FAIL rows to the CISO office channel; throttle per `measure`. Routing targets (SOC, compliance team, specific queue) must be documented with named on-call owners; an 'alerts go to the team' runbook fails at 3am on Sunday.

• **Hint 5:** Archive CSV/PDF outputs to `index=audit_evidence` with signing per `evidenceSigning`. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

The detection fires when `health_score >= 70`. For "NIS2 Effectiveness Assessment of Cybersecurity Measures," the aggregation produces the risk posture — the per-row values should reconcile to the organisation's risk register entries, not stand alone as engineering metrics. This threshold is the operational heart of the control — set it too low and the signal drowns in noise; set it too high and real compliance deviations go undetected. The value `70` should never be treated as universal; it must be calibrated against your specific operational baseline.

Calibration procedure: before enabling this as a production alert, run the base search over the last 30 days with the threshold removed and compute distribution statistics:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats count by health_score
| stats avg(health_score) as mean, perc50(health_score) as p50, perc90(health_score) as p90, perc99(health_score) as p99, max(health_score) as hi
```

Set the production threshold between p90 and p99 depending on your organisation's alert fatigue tolerance. Document the chosen value in the control specification alongside the date of calibration and the sample size — auditors will ask how you arrived at `70` and the defensible answer is 'data, plus a documented review by the control owner,' not 'the template said so.'

The `dc()` aggregation over the last 24 hours is a deliberate choice. Compliance reports on sustained behaviour, not instantaneous spikes — a single noisy event crossing the threshold for one second should not page the on-call. If your organisation requires point-in-time sensitivity (e.g. real-time fraud detection), shorten the time window and aggregation span; for accountability reporting, widen them. Tune for the regulation's actual expectation, not engineering preference.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.2.9: NIS2 Effectiveness Assessment of Cybersecurity Measures]
cron_schedule = 0 1 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.2.9,reg=NIS2
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=_internal sourcetype=splunk_web_access earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `age_days`, `in_sla`, `measure`, `mfa_users`, `pct`, `pct_raw`, `sla_days`, `status`, `target`, `total_users`. Run:

```spl
index=_internal sourcetype=splunk_web_access earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table age_days, in_sla, measure, mfa_users, pct, pct_raw, sla_days, status
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `health_score`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(health_score) as p50 perc90(health_score) as p90 perc99(health_score) as p99 max(health_score) as hi
```

The current threshold `70` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.2.9: NIS2 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Kpi Tiles (`pass`, `fail per measure`):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Gauges (`% vs target`):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Sortable Table Of Failing Measures (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 4 — Line Chart Of Rolling Quarter Trends (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Too many or too few alerts:** The threshold is almost certainly miscalibrated for your estate — re-run the percentile calibration (Step 3d) on a representative 30-day window and pick a value between p90 and p99.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**NIS2-specific failure modes:**

• 24/7 reporting obligation (Art. 23): NIS2 requires early warning within 24 hours of awareness of a significant incident. Detections that back this SLA must compute elapsed time from the SOC's first-acknowledged timestamp (not the resolve-time) and must trigger independently of business hours — the regulatory clock does not pause overnight.

• Member-state variation: NIS2 is transposed individually by each EU Member State with some latitude on thresholds and sector scoping. A detection correct for German IT-SiG 3 (BSI) may be misaligned with Dutch CWV or French LPM. Use `nis2_member_state_config.csv` to parametrize thresholds per jurisdiction; never hardcode.

• Essential vs Important entity criteria: the applicable obligations depend on sector + size. Before alerting, verify the monitored entity's designation; a detection firing on an out-of-scope subsidiary causes unnecessary regulatory notifications that erode trust with the national CSIRT.

• Supply-chain (Art. 21(2)(d)) extent: NIS2 explicitly includes direct suppliers' cybersecurity posture. Detections must cover monitored-by-you-not-owned-by-you infrastructure (outsourced SaaS, managed SIEM tenants) — these are in scope even if operationally run by a third party. BA/BAA-style governance applies.
grandmaExplanation: We check a few simple health scores—how fast serious software flaws get fixed, whether important services stay in good shape, and a rough sign that people are signing in the safer way—so we can show outsiders our protections actually work week to week.
